name: Tanuki
description: The Tanuki, also known as the bake-danuki, is a prominent figure in Japanese folklore and mythology. Often depicted as jovial and mischievous shapeshifters, these raccoon dogs are believed to possess magical abilities, most notably the power to transform into other objects or people. Their iconic appearance, often portrayed with a large belly and an oversized scrotum, has made them a popular symbol of good luck and prosperity. While sometimes depicted as tricksters, Tanuki are generally benevolent creatures, known for their love of sake and their playful nature. They are associated with wealth and are often found depicted with promissory notes or sake bottles. Their image is prevalent in Japanese art, literature, and popular culture, solidifying their place as beloved and recognizable figures in the Japanese cultural landscape. Tanuki embody the spirit of nature and the unpredictable, often humorous, aspects of the supernatural world.
subtitle: Mischievous shapeshifting raccoon dog of Japanese folklore.
appearance: Tanuki are raccoon dogs with reddish-brown fur, short legs, and a bushy tail. They are often depicted wearing a straw hat and carrying a sake bottle. Their most distinctive feature is their disproportionately large scrotum, which is often used in their shapeshifting and magical abilities.
behavior: Tanuki are generally playful and mischievous creatures, known for their love of sake and their ability to trick humans. While they can be mischievous, they are not usually malicious and are often seen as benevolent tricksters.

keyMyths.title: The Tanuki's Tea Kettle
keyMyths.description: A monk discovers a tea kettle that is actually a Tanuki in disguise. The Tanuki, unable to maintain its form, reveals its true nature but continues to perform tricks and entertain the monk, eventually bringing him good fortune through performances.

keyMyths.title: Tanuki and the Woodcutter
keyMyths.description: A kind woodcutter helps a trapped Tanuki, and in return, the Tanuki tries to repay him with gifts of leaves disguised as gold. However, the gold turns back into leaves, highlighting the Tanuki's well-intentioned but sometimes flawed magic.

keyMyths.title: Tanuki's Drum
keyMyths.description: Tanuki are known for drumming on their bellies. In some stories, the sound is used to lure unsuspecting travelers or to create illusions and distractions. This drumming is a signature characteristic associated with Tanuki lore.

sources.author: Hearn, Lafcadio
sources.work: Kwaidan: Stories and Studies of Strange Things
sources.description: A collection of Japanese ghost stories and folklore, including tales featuring Tanuki.
sources.text: A collection of Japanese ghost stories and folklore, including tales featuring Tanuki.

sources.work: The Book of Yokai: Mysterious Creatures of Japanese Folklore
